title: Customer Concentration: The Exit Discount Most Business Owners Don't See Coming
excerpt: When buyers examine an Australian business for sale, customer concentration is one of the first risks they price into the offer. Here is what that calculation looks like and how to change the outcome.
content:
Closing a strong financial year with a major client renewing for another term feels like the business is working exactly as it should. Revenue is predictable, the relationship is solid, and the numbers support it. What that picture does not show is how a buyer reads the same situation during due diligence: not as evidence of strength, but as a concentration of risk.

Customer concentration is one of the most consistently underpriced factors in private business transactions in Australia. Not because owners ignore their client mix, but because the reliance tends to accumulate through success. You service the anchor client well. They grow. Their share of your revenue grows with them. Each year it feels less like a vulnerability and more like a foundation.

A buyer does not see a foundation. A buyer sees a single point of failure and prices the offer accordingly.

What Buyers Map in the First Week of Due Diligence

Among the first things any experienced buyer or their advisers analyse is a breakdown of revenue by customer. Specifically, they look at three numbers: the percentage of total revenue coming from the single largest customer, the top three, and the top five. These figures do not need to be buried in a spreadsheet; they are visible in most accounting software with a single report.

The threshold at which institutional buyers typically flag elevated concentration risk is when one customer accounts for more than 20 per cent of revenue. At 30 per cent, it becomes a deal structuring issue rather than a risk note. Above that level, some buyers walk away. Others proceed, but the deal mechanics change.

The underlying concern is straightforward: if the anchor client leaves, reduces spend, or renegotiates terms in the months after settlement, the buyer has paid for a business that no longer exists in the form they priced. That gap between the sale price and the actual value received is not something buyers leave unaddressed in the offer.

Where the Discount Shows Up in the Deal

The customer concentration discount rarely appears as a named line item in an offer. It shows up in the structure of the deal itself, in ways that can look like standard commercial negotiation until you recognise the pattern. The three most common expressions are:

  • A lower earnings multiple than comparable transactions in the same sector, applied to the whole business rather than just the at-risk revenue
  • An earnout structure where a portion of the purchase price is contingent on the anchor client relationship continuing for one to two years post-settlement
  • A condition precedent requiring written confirmation from the anchor client before the deal can close

Each of these mechanisms transfers concentration risk back to the seller, either by reducing the headline price or by making part of the proceeds conditional on someone else's decision. The

>Exit Readiness Diagnostic that ProfitPulse conducts scores businesses across eight buyer-grade readiness dimensions, and customer concentration is consistently among those that produce the widest gap between what owners anticipate and what a buyer's model reflects.

What Changes the Outcome Before You Exit

The most effective response to concentration risk is time, applied deliberately. Twelve to twenty-four months of focused effort on building the second and third tiers of the client base changes how a buyer reads the same revenue structure entirely.

A long-term contract with the anchor client helps, but it does not resolve the concentration itself. A buyer still values the business based on what the earnings look like when that contract expires. What actually shifts the assessment is a second client growing to between fifteen and twenty per cent of revenue, reducing the top-customer share not by shrinking the anchor relationship but by building alongside it.

Documenting the commercial relationship also matters. Buyers apply a steeper discount to anchor relationships they believe are built on personal rapport with the current owner rather than on contracts, recurring fee structures, or embedded systems. If the client relationship looks portable across a change of ownership, the buyer's confidence in the revenue rising. If it looks owner-dependent, the departure risk in their model stays elevated regardless of how long the client has been with you.

For businesses actively preparing for an exit, or that want to understand where they stand ahead of one, a

>Customer Concentration and Profitability Map provides a clear picture of the current position: which clients are genuinely sticky, which relationships carry departure risk, and where the concentration sits before a buyer identifies it instead.

The businesses that attract the cleanest exit terms are rarely those with the highest revenue. They are the ones where no single client, no single contract, and no single relationship is bearing too much of the commercial weight. That balance takes time to build. With a new financial year beginning in five days, there is no sharper prompt to measure where you are than the client list sitting behind this month's final accounts.

faqs.q: What level of customer concentration concerns buyers in an Australian business sale?
faqs.a: When one customer accounts for more than 20 per cent of revenue, most experienced buyers flag the concentration and adjust their deal terms. At 30 per cent, it typically becomes a structural issue that shapes how the offer is written, not just a risk note in the report. The specific threshold varies by industry and deal size, but the underlying concern is consistent: what does the earnings picture look like if that client's contribution reduces after settlement?

faqs.q: How does customer concentration affect business valuation multiples in Australia?
faqs.a: Customer concentration reduces valuation in two main ways. The first is a lower earnings multiple, applied to reflect the risk that a portion of revenue may not transfer reliably to new ownership. The second is deal structuring, where an earnout or condition transfers some of the concentration risk back to the seller rather than reducing the headline price outright. Understanding the factors that shape your business valuation helps identify which risks are addressable before a sale process begins.

faqs.q: Can I still sell my business if one client is more than 30 per cent of revenue?
faqs.a: Yes, but the deal terms often look different. Buyers may apply a lower multiple, require an earnout tied to the client relationship continuing post-settlement, or include a condition precedent that the anchor client confirms in writing they will remain. In some cases buyers apply a normalised earnings figure that discounts the at-risk revenue before calculating the offer price. None of these outcomes are automatic, but they become more likely when concentration is visible and not addressed before the process begins.

faqs.q: How long does it take to reduce customer concentration before selling a business?
faqs.a: Typically 12 to 24 months of deliberate effort, starting from the point where the concentration is clearly identified. The most effective approach is growing the second and third tiers of the client base without reducing the anchor relationship, until no single client bears a disproportionate share of the revenue risk. The earlier this work begins, the more time there is to demonstrate a trend in the numbers, which matters as much to buyers as the position at the point of sale.

faqs.q: What is an earnout and why does it appear in deals with customer concentration risk?
faqs.a: An earnout is a deal structure where part of the purchase price is held back and paid only if specific conditions are met after settlement. In concentration-related transactions, this typically means the deferred amount is paid if the anchor client relationship continues for one to two years post-close. It transfers concentration risk to the seller rather than reducing the headline price, which can appear reasonable but leaves a portion of the proceeds dependent on a decision made by someone other than the buyer or seller.

faqs.q: What does the Exit Readiness Diagnostic cover for Queensland and NSW business owners?
faqs.a: The Exit Readiness Diagnostic scores a business across eight buyer-grade dimensions: financial quality, contracts, customer concentration, owner dependence, systems and processes, team depth, growth story, and risk profile. Most owner-operators find that concentration and owner dependence produce the widest gap between their internal view of the business and what a buyer would actually pay. The diagnostic takes approximately two weeks and delivers a scored report with prioritised actions across each dimension.
